coredata.dc:title: Transforming water-soluble carbon dots with high particle size regularity as the high-performance oil-based lubricant additives by one-step surface modification
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Zhenru
Zhao, Jianquan
Li, Xuan
Wang, Baogang
coredata.dc:description: Developing high-performance carbon dot (CD)-based lubricant additives towards non-polar base oils is always challenging because it is an arduous task to gain hydrophobic CDs with high quality. In this work, with the aid of the chemical reaction between hydroxyl groups and lauroyl chloride molecules, the water-soluble CDs-OH derived from the solvothermal method with particularly high particle size regularity were transformed as the oil-soluble CDs-LA by the one-step surface modification process. As expected, the CDs-LA capped by abundant long alkyl chain groups exhibited excellent long-term dispersion stability in non-polar base oil of polyalphaolefin (PAO4) and hence could be served as the additives of PAO4 directly. Under the same test conditions, the tribological performance of CDs-LA was obviously superior to the hydrophobic CDs obtained by the pyrolysis method, implying that the high particle size regularity was beneficial for making full use of the lubrication functions of CDs. Loading 1.7 wt% of CDs-LA made the friction and wear of PAO4 reduced by 32.2 % and 76.4 %, respectively. When the duration prolonged from 20 min to 200 min, the friction reduction of base oil triggered by CDs-LA further enlarged to 46.9 %. The friction-reducing and anti-wear performance of CDs-LA under high loads was also satisfactory. The worn surface analysis results demonstrated that the robust and homogenous boundary lubrication films composed of FexOy and CDs have generated on the rubbing surfaces lubricated by CDs-LA/PAO4 dispersion, well accounting for the outstanding friction reduction and wear resistance functions of CDs-LA.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot
Particle size regularity
Lubricant additive
Surface modification
Friction reduction
Wear resistance

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Tribological tests and wear track surface analyses

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors thank the financial support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51975493 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Facile synthesis of oil-soluble CDs-LA from water-soluble CDs-OH.
TEM and high-resolution TEM images of (a, b) CDs-OH and (c, d) CDs-LA. Insets of (a) and (b): particle size distribution diagram and corresponding SAED pattern of CDs-OH.
(a) FTIR spectra and (b) TGA curves of CDs-OH and CDs-LA.

(a) Optical photos of CDs-LA/PAO4 dispersion (1.7 wt%) under room and UV light (λ = 365 nm) after holding for 30 days at 25, −15 and 150 °C or centrifuging for 20 min at 10000 rpm (neat base oil of PAO4 at 25 °C was used as reference and listed as the leftmost sample). (b) Absorbance of CDs-LA/PAO4 dispersion (1.7 wt%) at 365 nm varying with the increasing holding time.
(a) CoF curves, (b) mean COF and WV of CDs-LA/PAO4 dispersion under the load of 40 N and duration of 20 min as a function of loading concentration (c, wt%).
(a) Mean COF and (b) SPR of PAO4 and CDs-LA/PAO4 dispersion (1.7 wt%) as a function of load (duration: 20 min).

(a) CoF curves, (b) mean COF and WV of PAO4 and CDs-LA/PAO4 dispersion (1.7 wt%) under the load of 40 N and duration of 200 min. Insets of (a): the 3D profiles of wear track lubricated by dispersion at the durations of 4000 and 10,000 s, respectively.
Optical microscopic photos and the corresponding 3D and 2D profiles of wear tracks lubricated by (a) PAO4 and (b) CDs-LA/PAO4 dispersion (1.7 wt%) under the load of 40 N and duration of 20 min.

SEM images and the corresponding selected area (marked out by the white boxes) elemental mappings including atomic contents of wear tracks lubricated by (a) PAO4 and (b) CDs-LA/PAO4 dispersion (1.7 wt%) under the load of 40 N and duration of 20 min.
XPS Fe2p, C1s and O1s spectra of wear tracks lubricated by (a) PAO4 and (b) CDs-LA/PAO4 dispersion (1.7 wt%) under the load of 40 N and duration of 20 min.
Schematic illustration of lubrication mechanism of CDs-LA as additives.
The specific test conditions of frictional tests.

head.ce:title: Transforming water-soluble carbon dots with high particle size regularity as the high-performance oil-based lubricant additives by one-step surface modification

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest Petroleum University, Chengdu 610500, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest Petroleum University, Chengdu 610500, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Developing high-performance carbon dot (CD)-based lubricant additives towards non-polar base oils is always challenging because it is an arduous task to gain hydrophobic CDs with high quality. In this work, with the aid of the chemical reaction between hydroxyl groups and lauroyl chloride molecules, the water-soluble CDs-OH derived from the solvothermal method with particularly high particle size regularity were transformed as the oil-soluble CDs-LA by the one-step surface modification process. As expected, the CDs-LA capped by abundant long alkyl chain groups exhibited excellent long-term dispersion stability in non-polar base oil of polyalphaolefin (PAO4) and hence could be served as the additives of PAO4 directly. Under the same test conditions, the tribological performance of CDs-LA was obviously superior to the hydrophobic CDs obtained by the pyrolysis method, implying that the high particle size regularity was beneficial for making full use of the lubrication functions of CDs. Loading 1.7 wt% of CDs-LA made the friction and wear of PAO4 reduced by 32.2 % and 76.4 %, respectively. When the duration prolonged from 20 min to 200 min, the friction reduction of base oil triggered by CDs-LA further enlarged to 46.9 %. The friction-reducing and anti-wear performance of CDs-LA under high loads was also satisfactory. The worn surface analysis results demonstrated that the robust and homogenous boundary lubrication films composed of FexOy and CDs have generated on the rubbing surfaces lubricated by CDs-LA/PAO4 dispersion, well accounting for the outstanding friction reduction and wear resistance functions of CDs-LA.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: During the past decade, the carbon dots (CDs) with particle size lower than 10 nm are emerging as the most dazzling stars in the lubricant additive field [1–3]. The standing out of CDs from numerous nanoadditives can be attributed to their unique advantages such as environmental friendliness, good comparability with base oils, high thermal and chemical stability, and strong film-building ability on the rubbing surfaces. The most of carbon sources for synthesizing CDs are organic small molecules usually containing C, H, O and N elements, which ensure the environmental friendliness of CDs [4]. The bottom-up synthesis methods for CDs such as the pyrolysis, solvothermal, microwave, oxidation and ultrasonic methods endow them with the rich surface groups and hence good comparability with base oils [5]. The carbonization is one of the key procedures for synthesizing CDs, which guarantees the high thermal and chemical stability of CDs [6]. Actually, the strong film-building ability on the friction interfaces have been the core competence of CDs as additives, which directly determines the extraordinary tribological performance of CDs and is correlated with many parameters such as small but regular particle size, long-term dispersion stability in base oil and the synergistic effect of carbon core and surface group [7].

As a certain amount of oxygen-containing groups will inevitably survive on the surfaces of CDs during the synthesis process, the CDs have been widely used as the additives of polar lubricants such as the water and polyethylene glycol (PEG200) [8–11]. Loading low amounts of CDs tremendously improved the tribological performance of the PEG200 [12]. On one hand, the ionic liquids [13–16], polymers [17–19], organic molecules [20], polyelectrolytes [21–23] and poly(ionic liquid)s [24] have been used as the modifiers to regulate the performance CD-based additives. On the other hand, heteroatom doped CD-based additives were also gained [25,26]. For example, adding poly(ionic liquid)s brushes grafted CDs (1.5 wt%) made the friction and wear of PEG200 reduced by 61.9 and 82.5 % under the ball-on-disk sliding mode [27]. The friction and wear reductions of PEG200 triggered by ionic liquids inserted CDs (1.0 wt%) further increased to 72.7 and 96.5 % under the four-ball friction mode [28]. The tribological performance of CDs as additives of PEG200 is superior to or comparable to other kinds of nanoadditives such as metals, oxides, graphene and composites [29–34]. The nearly spherical morphology of CDs with small but regular particle size was beneficial to highlighting their rolling, mending and polishing on the rubbing surfaces. The rich functional groups on CDs enhanced their dispersion stability in PEG200 and embedding stability between friction interfaces. The robust and homogenous boundary lubrication films with high durability, striking thickness in hundreds of nanometer and amazing self-lubricating feature were formed on the rubbing surfaces lubricated with the PEG200 containing CDs [22], well accounting for the distinguished friction reduction and wear resistance functions of CDs.

The unavoidability of oxygen-containing groups on surfaces of CDs brought in huge difficult for synthesizing and purifying of hydrophobic CDs, thereby greatly hindering the application of CDs as additives of non-polar base oil such as polyalphaolefin (PAO). Up to now, the main strategy to gain oil-soluble CDs as additives of PAO is covalently attaching hydrophobic groups onto the surfaces of CDs by pyrolysis of precursors and modifiers [35–39]. The biggest drawback for this strategy was the relatively poor quality of CDs including bad particle size regularity, low modification rate and high content of impurities, which largely influenced the tribological performance of CDs [35–39]. With the help of dispersant or solvation effect [40,41], the amphipathic CDs were also accidentally used as additives of PAO. However, these two means were difficult to popularize because specific CDs, dispersant and solvent were necessary. In addition, the dosage of dispersant and long-term dispersion stability of amphipathic CDs in PAO under the relatively harsh conditions needed to be considered. Whatever, the development of high-performance CD-based additives towards PAO was always challenging.

In this work, the high-quality CDs with abundant hydroxyl groups (CDs-OH) were synthesized by the solvothermal method (Fig. 1 ). Subsequently, the water-soluble CDs-OH were transformed as the oil-soluble CDs (CDs-LA) by one-step surface modification based on the reaction between hydroxyl and acyl chloride groups. The CDs-LA with excellent dispersion stability in PAO4 was served as the additives of PAO4 and their tribological performance were evaluated systematically. The lubrication mechanism of CDs-LA was explored based on characterizing the worn surfaces. This work can not only provide a reliable strategy to acquire oil-soluble CDs with high-quality, but also promote the application process of CD-based additives towards non-polar base oils.

Glucose (99 %), ethanol (99.7 %), dichloromethane (99.5 %), ether (99.5 %), methanol (99.5 %) and triethylamine (99 %) with analytical purity were purchased from Chengdu Kelong Chemicals Co., Ltd. (China). Lauroyl chloride (98 %) was bought from Aladdin Shanghai Chemical Regent Co., Ltd. (China). The base oil of polyalphaolefin (PAO4) was provided by the Mobil. All the chemicals were used as received without further treatments.

First of all, the CDs-OH shown in Fig. 1 were synthesized according to the reported solvothermal method with a few modifications [42]. Briefly, 2.0 g of glucose and 30 mL of ethanol were added to a 100 mL reactor with a polytetrafluoroethylene liner. Then, the reactor was placed in a muffle furnace, heated to 180 °C and kept at this temperature for 12 h. After the muffle furnace was cooled to room temperature, the dark brown suspension in the reactor was filtered by a filter membrane (0.22 μm) to remove the big particles and obtain a clear solution-like liquid. The filtrate was dialyzed by a dialysis bag (1000 Da) in regularly changed ultrapure water for 3–4 days. Most of solvent in the dialysate was removed by the rotary evaporation. Finally, the CDs-OH were obtained as a brown powder by the freeze-drying.

Then, the oil-soluble CDs-LA derived from the CDs-OH were synthesized by one-step surface modification (Fig. 1). 0.1 g of CDs-OH, 6 mL of triethylamine and 30 mL of dichloromethane were orderly added into a 100 mL round bottom flask under magnetic stirring. 24 mL of lauryl chloride solution in dichloromethane (25 wt%) was added into the above flask drop by drop under the room temperature. After the dropping process was complete, the reaction in flask continuously lasted for 12 h. Then, the solvent in the above reaction system was removed by rotary evaporation to obtain the white floccule and black viscous materials. To remove the above white floccule (i.e., triethylamine hydrochloride), the above mixture was extracted by 3 batches of ether (10 mL). The ether in the red-brown extract liquid was eliminated by a rotary evaporator to obtain a black viscous substance. The viscous substance was washed by 3 batches of methanol (10 mL) to remove other impurities derived from the excess lauryl chloride. Finally, the CDs-LA were obtained as a black oily sample after drying in a vacuum oven at 60 °C.

The instruments including the transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Tecnai G20), Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, WQF-520) and thermal gravimetric analyzer (TGA, SDTA85) were used to characterize the microstructure, morphology, surface groups and thermal stability of various CDs. The TEM samples were prepared by adding a drop of CDs-OH/ethanol suspension or CDs-LA/dichloromethane suspension (0.02 mg/mL) onto a copper grid coated with a carbon layer and drying at room temperature. FTIR spectra were obtained by the KBr pellet method. The TGA curves were gained under the N2 environment at a heating rate of 10 °C/min over a temperature range of 40 to 800 °C. The UV–vis absorbance spectra of CDs-LA/PAO4 suspension (1.7 wt%) standing for different times were recorded on a UV–vis spectrophotometer (U-4100).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Tribological tests and wear track surface analyses
ce:section.ce:para: Tribological properties of CDs-LA as the additives of PAO4 were evaluated on a Bruker universal mechanical tester (UMT) platform under the ball-on-disk reciprocating sliding mode and steel/steel contact. The upper running ball (12.7 mm in diameter) and the lower stationary disk (50 mm × 35 mm × 4 mm in size) were both made of AISI 52100 bearing steel with the hardness of about 59–61 HRC. The specific test conditions of frictional tests are listed in Table 1 . All the tests were conducted at room temperature and under atmospheric environment. The balls and disks were ultrasonically cleaned in acetone for 10 min prior to the tests. Then 0.2 mL of PAO4 containing CDs-LA or not was dropped onto the ball-disk contact region. The coefficients of friction (COF) were recorded continuously by a linked computer. Each frictional test was repeated for at least three times. The mean COF was calculated as the average value of all data points on the friction coefficient curve. After the frictional test, the lower disk was cleaned in acetone by ultrasonic treatment. The two- and three-dimensional profiles of wear tracks of the lower disks were gained by Bruker Contour GT-K 3D optical microscope equipped with a white-light interferometer. The wear volume (WV) of lower disk was calculated based on the integral area of two-dimensional profile and the amplitude. The specific wear rates (SWR) of the worn surface were calculated using the eq. K = V/SF, where V was the wear volume (mm3), S was the total sliding distance (m), and F was the normal load (N). The morphology and elemental composition of the worn surface was analyzed by a JSM-7500F scanning electron microscope (SEM) with an energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscope (EDS) and an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (ESCALAB 250) with a mono-chromatized Al Ka X-ray source (1486.71 eV).

The TEM image (Fig. 2a) confirms that the spherical CDs-OH have been successfully synthesized by the solvothermal method in ethanol. The particle size distribution diagram (inset of Fig. 2a) shows that almost all the synthesized CDs-OH are located at 2–4 nm in size, reflecting the particularly high particle size regularity of CDs-OH. The average particle size of CDs-OH is calculated as 3.0 nm by counting more than 100 particles. The TEM images with high-resolution (Fig. 2b) indicate that the CDs-OH are almost amorphous as the typical lattice structures of graphite can be hardly found. The obscure halos shown in selected-area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern (inset of Fig. 2b) further verify the above viewpoint. The TEM images of CDs-LA shown in Fig. 2c and d demonstrate that the morphology of CDs-LA is very similar to CDs-OH. In other words, the surface modification has brought in negligible morphology vibration for CDs-OH.

In the FTIR spectrum of CDs-OH (Fig. 3a), the absorption band at 3442 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibrations of OH, directly revealing the rich –OH groups on the surfaces of CDs-OH. The bands at 1707 and 1630 cm−1 can be due to the CO and CC stretching vibrations, respectively, implying that there are also few other oxygen-containing groups such as aldehyde groups on the surfaces of CDs-OH. In the FTIR spectrum of CDs-LA, the absorption bands at 2925 and 2856 cm−1 derived from CH stretching vibrations of –CH2– and –CH3– groups appear, suggesting the existence of long alkyl chain groups on the surfaces of CDs-LA. The band at 1466 cm−1 caused by the CH deformation vibrations of alkyl chains further confirms the above viewpoint. The bands at 1875 and 1723 cm−1 can be assigned to the CO stretching vibrations of anhydride and ester groups. At the same time, it is worth noting that the bands belonging to –OH and aldehyde groups are disappeared, meaning that the reactions between oxygen-containing groups of CDs-OH and acyl chloride groups of lauroyl chloride were taken place during the surface modification process (Fig. 1). These FTIR results jointly verify that the long alkyl chain groups have been covalently attached onto the surfaces of CDs-LA by ester groups and slight anhydride groups.

The TGA curve of CDs-OH shown in Fig. 3b illustrates a gradual weight loss process with the rising temperature from 40 to 800 °C, which can be attributed to the moisture volatilization, thermal composition of oxygen-containing groups and sublimation of amorphous substance. According to the TGA curve of CDs-LA (Fig. 3b), the remarkable weight loss begins at the temperature higher than 150 °C because of the thermal composition of long alkyl chain groups and sublimation of amorphous substance. The remaining weight for CDs-LA is only about 5 % after the heating process, whereas it is about 30 % for CDs-OH, revealing that a relatively high modification rate was achieved by the one-step surface modification process shown in Fig. 1.

Prior to evaluating the tribological performance of CDs-LA as additives of PAO4, the dispersion stability of CDs-LA in base oil was investigated. As shown in Fig. 4a, the CDs-LA/PAO4 dispersion (1.7 wt%) under room light always presents as the transparent solution-like fluid with an olive brown color after holding for 30 days at 25, −15 and 150 °C or centrifuging for 20 min at 10,000 rpm, intuitively reflecting the excellent long-term dispersion stability of CDs-LA in base oil. Moreover, the CDs-LA/PAO4 dispersion (1.7 wt%) under UV light emits the bright blue florescence all the time, meaning that the CDs-LA can barely form aggregates after holding for a long time under common or harsh conditions. The absorbance of CDs-LA/PAO4 dispersion (1.7 wt%) at 365 nm shown in Fig. 4b is almost constant with the increasing holding time, further confirming that no sedimentation or aggregation of CDs-LA happens in the dispersion. The superior long-period dispersion stability of CDs-LA in PAO4 can be attributed to the rich alkyl chains on the surfaces of CDs-LA, which offers great application potential for CDs-LA as lubricant additives of hydrophobic base oils.

The effects of loading concentration (c, wt%) on tribological performance of CDs-LA as additives of PAO4 were studied under the load of 40 N and duration of 20 min. According to the Fig. 5a, all the dispersions with various c exhibit remarkable friction-reducing function because their coefficient of friction (COF) curves are obviously lower and smoother than that of base oil. The sharp peaks at the start of the COF curves are triggered by the running-in periods of frictional tests. The subsequent rising of COF after the peak can be attributed to the slight lubrication failure phenomenon of base oil. The COF and WV values shown in Fig. 5b illustrate that the CDs-LA show the best friction-reducing and anti-wear performance at the c of 1.7 wt%. In comparison with PAO4, the COF and WV of CDs-LA/PAO4 dispersion reduce by 32.2 % and 76.4 %, respectively. Under the same conditions, the hexadecylamine modified CDs (CDs-HA) synthesized by the pyrolysis method in our previous work made the COF and WV of PAO4 reduce by 27.1 % and 45.9 %, respectively [35]. Undoubtedly, the CDs-LA reported here possess much better tribological performance than CDs-HA [35]. This phenomenon can be explained from the point of CD quality, that is, the CDs-LA synthesized by solvothermal method not only have a much higher particle size regularity but also contain a lower content of impurities compared with the CDs-HA synthesized by pyrolysis method.

At the optimum c of 1.7 wt%, the friction-reducing and anti-wear abilities of CDs-LA as additives under various loads were evaluated and shown in Fig. 6 . First, the mean COF and SWR of dispersion are always distinctly lower than that of base oil with the increasing load from 20 to 100 N, affirming the robust lubrication function of CDs-LA under different contact pressures. Secondly, the function of CDs-LA is attenuated to some extent when the load is increased. Typically, the mean COF and SWR differences between the PAO4 and dispersion at high loads (60, 80 and 100 N) are lower than that at low loads (20 and 40 N). At the load of 20 N, the CDs-LA exhibit the best friction-reducing property and bring in the biggest friction reduction of 42.3 % for base oil, which can be attributed to the highlighted rolling effect of CDs-LA as micro bearings at low load. Thirdly, the SPR of CDs-LA dispersion is obviously increased when the load increases from 40 N to 80 N, which can be attributed to the aggravation of adhesive wear with the increasing load. It is worth noting that the tribological performance of CDs-LA under high loads is still markedly superior to CDs-HA owing to their higher quality [35].

The tribological performance of CDs-LA/PAO4 dispersion (1.7 wt%) was also compared with the base oil by prolonging the test duration from 20 min to 200 min. As shown in Fig. 7a, the COF of PAO4 gradually rises at a high level with the increasing time, implying that the effective lubrication films can hardly generate on the rubbing surfaces during the friction process. Amazingly, the COF of dispersion keeps stable at a low level first during the beginning 5000 s and then suddenly declines to a lower level during the subsequent 7000 s, distinctly reflecting that the robust lubrication films must create on the rubbing surfaces during the friction process and the CDs-LA as additives should play an important role in the formation of lubrication films. The 3D profiles of wear track lubricated by the dispersion at 4000 and 10,000 s (insets of Fig. 7a) are very rough and smooth, respectively, further providing evidence for the above film-forming phenomenon. Correspondingly, the friction reduction (Fig. 7b) resulted in by CDs-LA further increases from 32.2 % to 46.9 % when the test duration prolongs from 20 min to 200 min. In addition, owing to the existence of lubrication films, the wear reduction shown in Fig. 7b (68.8 %) derived from the CDs-LA is still very eminent under the long duration of 200 min. Generally, the long-term lubricity of PAO4 can be promoted largely by loading CDs-LA as additives.

The lubrication mechanism of CDs-LA as additives was explored by characterizing the wear track surfaces lubricated by PAO4 and CDs-LA/PAO4 dispersion (1.7 wt%). The wear track lubricated by PAO4 (Fig. 8a) is wide and deep with some severe scratches, suggesting that the crucial abrasive and adhesive wears are existent between the friction pair owing to the insufficient lubricity of base oil. In comparison with the wear track lubricated by base oil, the width and depth of wear track lubricated by dispersion (Fig. 8b) both shrink obviously, directly revealing the distinguished anti-wear function of CDs-LA. According to the 3D profiles, the loading of CDs-LA into base oil also greatly smooths the surface of wear track, which can be attributed to the mending and polishing effects of CDs-LA. The smoother surface means slighter contact of friction pair, and hence resulting in more moderate friction and wear.

The eye-catching scratch and pit can be found in the SEM image of wear track surface lubricated by PAO4 (Fig. 9a), again confirming the existence of acute friction and wear between the ball and disk. In sharp contrast, the scratch and pit both disappear on the wear track lubricated by dispersion (Fig. 9b). Correspondingly, the completely smooth surface can be observed in Fig. 9b, which not only avoid the direct contact of friction pair, but also protect the rubbing surfaces from the serious friction and wear. By comparing the elemental contents of wear tracks lubricated by PAO4 and dispersion, the latter wear track contains much higher C and Fe contents but a lower O content, implying that the CDs-LA should play an important role in the formation of the above sediments and patch-like structures. In other words, the CDs-LA as additives trigger the generation of homogenous boundary lubrication films on the rubbing surfaces, which play the outstanding friction-reducing and anti-wear performance.

Fig. 10 gives out the XPS Fe2p, C1s and O1s spectra of wear tracks lubricated by base oil and CDs-LA/PAO4 dispersion (1.7 wt%). According to the Fe2p spectra, the main existence forms of Fe element on the two wear track surfaces are both iron oxides such as FeO and Fe2O3. Based on the C1s spectra, the main existence forms of C element on the two wear track surfaces are very similar owing to the oxidation of base oil during the friction process. The oxidation and thermal composition of PAO4 brings in some substances containing the same elemental composition of CDs-LA. In the O1s spectra, the content of FeO component on the former wear track is much higher than that on the latter wear track, signifying that the harsh oxidation reactions have taken place between the friction pair lubricated by the base oil. In the contrary, the oxidation degree of steel substrate is enormously alleviated on wear track lubricated by dispersion owing to the existence of boundary lubrication film induced by CDs-LA.

The lubrication mechanism of CDs-LA is discussed based on the above results of surface analysis. The base oil can barely provide enough lubrication as the oil film itself can hardly bear the extreme pressure contact of friction pair. Hence, the rubbing surfaces lubricated by PAO4 suffer severe wear and oxidation. As shown in Fig. 11 , the CDs-LA as additives can not only play the rolling, mending and polishing effects owing to their excellent dispersion stability in base oil, but also gradually smear onto the rubbing surfaces by deposition and sintering. Then, the robust tribofilms composed of FexOy and CDs-LA create on the rubbing surfaces, which protect the friction interfaces from direct contact, sharp friction and wear.

The oil-soluble CDs-LA were facile synthesized from the water-soluble CDs-OH with particularly high particle size regularity by one-step surface modification and served as the lubricant additives of PAO4. The rich long alkyl chain groups in CDs-LA endow them with excellent long-term dispersion stability in PAO4. At the optimum c of 1.7 wt%, the CDs-LA made the COF and WV of base oil reduce by 32.2 % and 76.4 %, respectively. When the duration prolonged from 20 min to 200 min, the COF reduction of base oil caused by CDs-LA further increased to 46.9 %. Although the tribological performance of CDs-LA attenuated to some extent under the high loads, their friction-reducing and anti-wear functions were still satisfactory. The results of worn surface analyses demonstrated that the robust and homogenous boundary lubrication films composed of FexOy and CDs-LA have generated on the rubbing surfaces lubricated by CDs-LA/PAO4 dispersion, which played the significant roles of friction reduction and wear resistance. Given their favorable environmental friendliness, excellent comparability with PAO4, outstanding friction-reducing and anti-wear performance, the CDs-LA are hopeful to become a kind of promising lubricant additives towards hydrophobic base oils.

Zhenru Zhang: Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Jianquan Zhao: Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation. Xuan Li: Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation. Baogang Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors confirm that they have no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the financial support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51975493).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Applications of carbon quantum dots in lubricant additives: a review
J. Mater. Sci.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Tang, Z. Zhang, Y. Li, Applications of carbon quantum dots in lubricant additives: a review, J. Mater. Sci. 56 (2021) 12061-12092.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Delivering quantum dots to lubricants: current status and prospect
Friction

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Zhang, T. Li, R. An, J. Wang, Y. Tian, Delivering quantum dots to lubricants: Current status and prospect, Friction 10 (2022) 1751-1771.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Functionalized carbon nanostructures as lubricant additives - a review
Carbon

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Nyholm, N. Espallargas, Functionalized carbon nanostructures as lubricant additives - A review, Carbon 201 (2023) 1200-1228.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Evolution and synthesis of carbon dots: from carbon dots to carbonized polymer dots
Adv. Sci.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Xia, S. Zhu, T. Feng, M. Yang, B. Yang, Evolution and synthesis of carbon dots: from carbon dots to carbonized polymer dots, Adv. Sci. 6 (2019) 1901316.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots: a new type of carbon-based nanomaterial with wide applications
ACS Cent. Sci.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Liu, R. Li, B. Yang, Carbon dots: a new type of carbon-based nanomaterial with wide applications, ACS Cent. Sci. 6 (2020) 2179-2195.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The light of carbon dots: from mechanism to applications
Matter

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Wang, S. Lu, The light of carbon dots: From mechanism to applications, Matter 5 (2022) 110-149.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fabrication of carbon dots intercalated MXene hybrids via laser treatment as oil-based additives for synergistic lubrication
Carbon

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ionic liquid capped carbon dots as a high-performance friction-reducing and antiwear additive for poly(ethylene glycol)
J. Mater. Chem. A
